intro: Exponentiation helps you describe repeated multiplication and fast growth, which shows up in patterns, science, and graphs that rise (or fall) quickly.

blocks.title: The core idea
blocks.content: In b^n, b is the base and n is the exponent. It means multiply b by itself n times (when n is a positive whole number).

blocks.title: Two key special exponents
blocks.content: Any nonzero number to the 0 power is 1, and any number to the 1 power is itself.

blocks.title: A quick picture
blocks.prompt: Compare how fast these grow: y=2^x and y=3^x. Notice both pass through (0,1).

blocks.title: Power rules (same base)
blocks.content: These rules help you simplify without expanding everything.

blocks.title: Quick examples
blocks.content: Add exponents when multiplying, subtract when dividing, and multiply exponents for a power of a power.

blocks.title: Try It Yourself
blocks.prompt: Change the base and see how the curve changes. Keep x between -3 and 3 so the graph stays readable.
blocks.challenge: Make a curve that grows faster than 2^x but slower than 4^x.

blocks.title: One real-life style example
blocks.content: If you double something each step, that’s a power of 2. For example, after 5 steps of doubling: 2^5 = 32.

mini_test.prompt: Which point is on the graph y = 2^x?
True or false: For any nonzero b, b^0 = 1.

answer_key.explanation: An exponent tells how many times to multiply the base by itself.
When multiplying same bases, add exponents: 3+5=8.
A power of a power multiplies exponents: 2\u00d73=6.
Any nonzero number to the 0 power equals 1.
Divide same bases by subtracting exponents: 7-2=5.
The zero-exponent rule says b^0=1 for b\u22600.
Adding equal powers doubles the value: 2^3+2^3=2\u00b72^3=2^4, not 2^6.
The correct rule is (a^m)^n=a^(mn), not a^(m+n).

subject: math

research_sources.title: Exponentiation
research_sources.snippet: Exponentiation Graphs of y = bx for various bases b: base 10, base e, base 2, base ⁠12⁠. Each curve passes through the point (0, 1) because any nonzero number raised to the power of 0 is 1. At x = 1, the value of y equals the base because any number raised to the power of 1 is the number itself. In mathematics, exponentiation, denoted bn, is an operation involving two numbers: the base, b, and the exponent or power, n. When n is a positive integer, exponentiation corresponds to repeated multiplication of the base: that is, bn is the product of multiplying n bases: b n = b × b × ⋯ × b × b ⏟ n times . {\displaystyle b^{n}=\underbrace {b\times b\times \dots \times b\times b} _{n{\text{ times...
